method.note: Three linear regression models fitted: NULL (intercept only), CAT (rainfall as categorical variable with K categories), and LOG (rainfall as continuous log-transformed predictor: mu = beta0 + beta1*ln(1+R)). Speed v modeled as Gaussian: V ~ N(mu, sigma^2).

Data: 5-min average driving speeds from HERE Europe GNSS floating car data on ~1.5 million road sections in Germany, combined with RADKLIM calibrated radar-gauge precipitation at 1km x 5min resolution. Analysis covers three summer days with heavy rainfall (20 May, 3 Jun, 12 Jun 2019).

Models estimated by ordinary least squares with clustered standard errors at the road-section level to account for within-section correlation. Relative speed difference delta_v_rel = (v - v0)/v0 used to normalize across road types.

Cross-validation: leave-one-day-out scheme (3-fold). Parameters estimated on 2 days, scored on the remaining day, repeated 3 times and averaged. Used to detect overfitting and compare CAT vs LOG model performance.

To detect potential overfitting in our models, cross-validation is app

method.note: Road sections stratified by speed limit (50, 70, 100, 130 km/h), lane category (single/multi), and road environment (urban/non-urban). Sections with tunnels, ramps, intersections, and persistent congestion excluded. Congestion defined as speed < 50% of speed limit in >10% of time steps.
For highway sections (multi-lane, 130 km/h, non-urban): 3,732,957 total speed observations, of which 265,040 (7.1%) had rainfall. Only 7,828 (0.2%) had rainfall > 1 L/m2 per 5 min.

Mean squared error skill score (MSESS) computed relative to the NULL model to quantify predictive benefit of including rainfall. MSESS computed both with and without cross-validation, and separately for all rainfall and heavy rainfall (>1 L/m2) conditions.

conclusion.note: Non-linear, approximately logarithmic decrease in driving speed with increasing rainfall, consistent with earlier studies. Logarithmic continuous predictor (LOG) outperforms categorical (CAT) model and avoids overfitting in sparse rainfall bins.

original_texts.text: The results indicate a non-linear, approximately logarithmic decrease

conclusion.note: Speed reductions range from ~2-10% under light rainfall / low speeds to >20% under heavy rainfall / high speeds. On highways (130 km/h), heavy rainfall >8 L/m2 in 5 min causes average speed reduction >30%. At 50 km/h, same rainfall gives only -8%.

original_texts.text: The estimated magnitudes of rainfall-related speed reduction

conclusion.note: Speed reductions systematically larger on roads with higher speed limits and on multi-lane roads. At 100 km/h non-urban, single-lane delta_v_rel = -15% vs multi-lane -26% at 8 L/m2 rainfall. Urban/non-urban differences are comparatively small for same speed limit.

Cross-validated MSESS_LOG = 0.135 on highways (13.5% MSE reduction). For heavy rainfall only (>1 L/m2), MSESS_LOG = 0.500 (50% MSE reduction). MSE reduction up to 14% on average across road types, up to 52.1% under heavy rainfall.

Incorporating rainfall as a predictor improves speed prediction, reducing cross-validated MSE by up to 14% on average, with substantially larger improvements under heavy rainfall. Consistent with ML-based studies reporting 1.5-25% improvements.

original_texts.text: Incorporating rainfall as a predictor improves speed prediction, reduc

conclusion.note: Results are event-based estimates from 3 days only; the analysis is explicitly described as a descriptive, event-specific study that does not support generalizable inference across time, seasons, or broader traffic conditions.

Speed reduction should NOT be interpreted as improved road safety. Rainfall increases crash risk through reduced visibility, adverse surface conditions, and altered traffic flow, even as drivers reduce speed.

innovation.note: First study to combine GNSS-based floating car data with calibrated radar-gauge rainfall (RADKLIM) at country scale (Germany) to model rainfall-speed relationships. Claims this as a first step toward country-scale, rainfall-aware speed modeling.

original_texts.text: combine GNSS-based floating car data with

innovation.note: Uses ~1.5 million road sections simultaneously, enabling comparison across diverse road types (speed limits, lanes, urban/non-urban) within a single study framework -- far broader spatial coverage than typical station-based approaches.

original_texts.text: This dataset offers a unique opportunity to investigate rainfall–speed

innovation.note: High spatio-temporal resolution data fusion: 1 km radar rainfall grid at 5-min intervals matched to 5-min average FCD speeds, enabling analysis of short-duration heavy rainfall effects that coarser data would miss.

original_texts.text: The average speed is computed based on all vehicles contributing a mea

innovation.note: Demonstrates that parsimonious, interpretable linear models (LOG) can capture systematic rainfall-speed relationships without complex ML approaches, while also showing LOG outperforms categorical treatment of rainfall.

limitation.note: Analysis based on only 3 summer days with heavy rainfall, limiting ability to capture seasonal effects, weekday variability, and diurnal traffic volume cycles. Results are event-based estimates, not long-term averages.

limitation.note: No direct traffic volume or congestion data available. Only a simple filtering heuristic applied (speed < 50% of limit in >10% of timesteps). Short-term congestion effects likely still present in data, confounding rainfall effects.

original_texts.text: direct traffic volume data were not available
Short-term congestion effects are likely still present

limitation.note: Intentionally simple linear regression approach. More complex frameworks (mixed-effects models, explicit temporal correlation structures) could better handle repeated observations, unobserved heterogeneity, and autocorrelation but were infeasible with available data.

original_texts.text: modeling approach was intentionally kept relatively simple

limitation.note: Residual clustering and temporal dependence remain in model errors, leading to potentially underestimated standard errors. Confidence intervals should be interpreted conservatively.

original_texts.text: confidence intervals should be interpreted conservatively

limitation.note: Rainfall assumed homogeneous within each 1 km radar grid cell. During convective events, sub-grid variability causes exposure misclassification at road-section level, likely attenuating estimated rainfall effects (conservative bias).

original_texts.text: homogeneous within each 1 km radar grid cell
speed reductions may represent conservative estimates

limitation.note: Only instantaneous rainfall effects analyzed; lagged effects (e.g., driving on wet roads after rain stops) not considered. RADKLIM cannot distinguish precipitation types (rain vs hail vs sleet vs snow).

limitation.note: No vehicle type information in GNSS probe data (cannot differentiate cars, trucks, etc.). Probe vehicle penetration rates may vary spatially and temporally, affecting representativeness.

Study does not assess traffic safety outcomes (crash occurrence, injury risk, mortality). Speed reduction should not be interpreted as evidence of improved road safety due to countervailing mechanisms.

limitation.note: Transferability unclear: results based on German road network with specific characteristics. Direct numerical comparisons across studies are difficult due to differences in data resolution, road types, and rainfall representation.
